chunk_summary: Initial management of suspected meningitis in SAM children includes emergency stabilization, empiric antibiotics for 14–21 days, and monitoring for complications like hypoglycemia and hypothermia.
learning_objectives: Recognize clinical signs of meningitis in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Understand the emergency stabilization steps for suspected meningitis in SAM.
Learn the recommended antibiotic regimen and duration for suspected meningitis in SAM.
Identify key monitoring priorities and complications in SAM children with meningitis.
key_facts: Suspect meningitis in SAM children with coma, convulsions, bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness, or altered sensorium.
Empiric antibiotic therapy includes a third-generation cephalosporin (cefotaxime or ceftriaxone) with an aminoglycoside (e.g., amikacin) for 14–21 days.
Avoid routine fluid boluses unless shock criteria are met to prevent overhydration.
Perform lumbar puncture and cultures before antibiotics if the child is stable; defer if unstable.
Monitor mental status, seizure recurrence, temperature, glucose, and respiratory trends closely.
Start F-75 feeding as soon as the airway is protected; use NG feeding if oral intake is unsafe.
reference_quote: Treat this as an emergency because delayed therapy has high mortality and clinical signs may be atypical. (Source: WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children)
document_key_topics: Emergency management of suspected meningitis in SAM
Monitoring and complications in SAM children with meningitis
